topics.meaning: A chemical reaction is a process in which one or more substances change into new substances with different properties.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Whenever a chemical change occurs we can say a chemical reaction has taken place. In a chemical reaction the nature and identity of the initial substances change, and new substances with new properties are formed. We can recognise that a chemical reaction has occurred by observing changes such as a change in state, change in colour, evolution of a gas, change in temperature, or formation of a precipitate. Examples include the burning of a magnesium ribbon to form magnesium oxide and the souring of milk. The substances that take part in a reaction are called reactants and the new substances formed are called products. These observable signs help us decide whether a chemical reaction has happened.
topics.easyExplanation: A chemical reaction is when substances combine or break apart to form completely new substances, often shown by changes like colour, gas, or heat.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify reactants
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Note the substances you start with, for example magnesium and oxygen.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe the change
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Watch for signs like colour change, gas, heat, or a precipitate.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form new products
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The reactants rearrange to form new substances with new properties.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Confirm the reaction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If a new substance with different properties is formed, a chemical reaction has occurred.

examples: Burning of a magnesium ribbon in air forms white magnesium oxide powder.
Milk left at room temperature in summer turns sour due to a chemical reaction.

importantKeywords.definition: A substance that takes part in and is used up during a chemical reaction.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Magnesium and oxygen are reactants when magnesium burns.

importantKeywords.definition: A new substance formed as a result of a chemical reaction.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Magnesium oxide is the product when magnesium burns.

importantKeywords.definition: An insoluble solid that separates out from a solution during a reaction.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Lead iodide forms a yellow precipitate.

topics.quickSummary: A chemical reaction converts reactants into new products with different properties, and is identified by changes in state, colour, gas evolution, temperature, or formation of a precipitate.
revisionNotes: New substances form in a chemical reaction.
Reactants are used up; products are formed.
Signs: colour change, gas, heat, precipitate.

topics.meaning: A chemical equation is the symbolic representation of a chemical reaction using formulae of reactants and products.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A word equation describes a reaction using the names of reactants and products, with reactants on the left and products on the right of an arrow pointing towards the products. A chemical equation is a shorter way of writing this using chemical symbols and formulae, for example the reaction of magnesium with oxygen is written as Mg + O2 -> MgO. The arrow shows the direction of the reaction. Such a representation is more concise and informative than words. Writing chemical formulae for the substances is essential before the equation can be balanced.
topics.easyExplanation: A chemical equation uses symbols and formulae to show what reacts and what is produced, with an arrow pointing from reactants to products.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the word equation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write reactant names on the left and product names on the right.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add an arrow
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Place an arrow pointing from reactants towards products.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Replace with formulae
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Substitute correct chemical symbols and formulae for each substance.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check completeness
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ensure every reactant and product is included.

examples: Hydrogen + Oxygen -> Water becomes 2H2 + O2 -> 2H2O.
Magnesium + Oxygen -> Magnesium oxide becomes 2Mg + O2 -> 2MgO.

importantKeywords.definition: A reaction written using the names of reactants and products.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Zinc + Sulphuric acid -> Zinc sulphate + Hydrogen.

importantKeywords.definition: An unbalanced chemical equation written with formulae.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Mg + O2 -> MgO is a skeletal equation.

importantKeywords.definition: A symbol showing the direction of the reaction towards products.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: In A + B -> C the arrow points to C.

topics.quickSummary: A chemical equation represents a reaction using symbols and formulae, with reactants on the left, products on the right, and an arrow showing the direction of change.
revisionNotes: Reactants on left, products on right.
Formulae are more concise than words.
Mg + O2 -> MgO is a skeletal equation.

topics.meaning: Balancing means making the number of atoms of each element equal on both sides of a chemical equation.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: According to the law of conservation of mass, mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction. This means the total mass of the reactants equals the total mass of the products, so the number of atoms of each element must be the same on both sides of the equation. An unbalanced or skeletal equation must therefore be balanced by placing suitable coefficients before the formulae, without changing the formulae themselves. The hit-and-trial method is used, adjusting coefficients element by element until both sides match. For example, 3Fe + 4H2O -> Fe3O4 + 4H2 is a balanced equation.
topics.easyExplanation: Balancing an equation makes sure each element has the same number of atoms on both sides, because atoms are never lost in a reaction.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the skeletal equation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: List reactants and products with correct formulae.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Count atoms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Count atoms of each element on both sides.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add coefficients
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Place numbers before formulae to equalise atoms, never changing formulae.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recheck
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Verify every element is balanced on both sides.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add state symbols
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write (s), (l), (g) or (aq) to indicate physical states.

examples: Fe + H2O -> Fe3O4 + H2 balances to 3Fe + 4H2O -> Fe3O4 + 4H2.
H2 + O2 -> H2O balances to 2H2 + O2 -> 2H2O.

importantKeywords.definition: Mass is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Total mass of reactants equals total mass of products.

importantKeywords.definition: A number placed before a formula to balance an equation.
An equation with equal atoms of each element on both sides.

topics.quickSummary: Equations are balanced by adding coefficients so that the number of atoms of each element is equal on both sides, satisfying the law of conservation of mass.
revisionNotes: Balance by adding coefficients, not changing formulae.
Mass of reactants = mass of products.

topics.meaning: A combination reaction is one in which two or more reactants combine to form a single product.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In a combination reaction two or more substances combine to form a single new substance. The burning of coal, where carbon combines with oxygen to form carbon dioxide, is an example. When quicklime (calcium oxide) reacts with water it forms slaked lime (calcium hydroxide) and releases a large amount of heat, so it is also an exothermic reaction. The reaction of hydrogen with oxygen to form water is another combination reaction. Many combination reactions are exothermic because heat is released as the new bonds form.
topics.easyExplanation: In a combination reaction, two or more things join together to make one new substance, often giving out heat.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify reactants
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find two or more substances that combine.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Combine them
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: They join to form a single product.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form one product
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Only one new substance is produced.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note energy change
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Many combination reactions release heat, making them exothermic.

examples: CaO + H2O -> Ca(OH)2 releases heat (quicklime to slaked lime).
C + O2 -> CO2 when coal burns.

importantKeywords.definition: A reaction where two or more reactants form one product.
Calcium oxide, CaO, which reacts vigorously with water.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Quicklime is used to make slaked lime.

importantKeywords.definition: A reaction that releases heat to the surroundings.

topics.quickSummary: In a combination reaction two or more reactants unite to form a single product, and such reactions are frequently exothermic, as when quicklime reacts with water.
revisionNotes: Two or more reactants form one product.
Burning of coal is a combination reaction.

topics.meaning: A decomposition reaction is one in which a single reactant breaks down to give simpler products.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In a decomposition reaction a single compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances. The energy needed for decomposition may be supplied as heat, light, or electricity, giving thermal decomposition, photodecomposition, and electrolytic decomposition respectively. For example, ferrous sulphate decomposes on heating to form ferric oxide, sulphur dioxide, and sulphur trioxide. Calcium carbonate decomposes on heating to calcium oxide and carbon dioxide, a reaction used in making cement. Silver chloride turns grey in sunlight as it decomposes into silver and chlorine, and water can be decomposed into hydrogen and oxygen by passing electricity. Decomposition reactions are usually endothermic because energy must be absorbed to break the compound apart.
topics.easyExplanation: A decomposition reaction is when one substance splits into two or more simpler substances using heat, light, or electricity.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Start with one reactant
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Begin with a single compound.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Supply energy
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Provide heat, light, or electricity.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Break it down
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The compound splits into simpler substances.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify the type
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Classify as thermal, photo, or electrolytic decomposition.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note energy absorbed
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Decomposition reactions are usually endothermic.

examples: CaCO3 -> CaO + CO2 on heating (thermal decomposition).
2AgCl -> 2Ag + Cl2 in sunlight (photodecomposition).

importantKeywords.definition: A reaction where one reactant breaks into simpler products.
Decomposition caused by heat.
A reaction that absorbs heat from the surroundings.

topics.quickSummary: A decomposition reaction breaks one reactant into simpler products using heat, light, or electricity, and is generally endothermic.

topics.meaning: A displacement reaction is one in which a more reactive element displaces a less reactive element from its compound.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In a displacement reaction a more reactive metal displaces a less reactive metal from its salt solution. For example, when an iron nail is placed in copper sulphate solution, the blue colour fades as iron displaces copper, forming iron sulphate and depositing copper. Zinc and lead are more reactive than copper, so they too can displace copper from its compounds. The reactivity series helps predict which metal will displace another. These reactions show that elements differ in their chemical reactivity.
topics.easyExplanation: In a displacement reaction, a more reactive metal pushes out a less reactive metal from its salt solution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Choose a reactive metal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pick a metal more reactive than the one in the compound.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add to salt solution
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Place it in the salt solution of the less reactive metal.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Displacement occurs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The reactive metal replaces the less reactive metal.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe products
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A new salt forms and the displaced metal is deposited.

examples: Fe + CuSO4 -> FeSO4 + Cu; the blue colour fades.
Zn + CuSO4 -> ZnSO4 + Cu.

importantKeywords.definition: A reaction where a more reactive element replaces a less reactive one.
A list of metals arranged by decreasing reactivity.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Zinc is above copper in the series.

importantKeywords.definition: A solution containing a dissolved salt of a metal.

topics.quickSummary: In a displacement reaction a more reactive element displaces a less reactive element from its salt solution, as when iron displaces copper from copper sulphate.
revisionNotes: More reactive metal displaces less reactive one.
Blue colour fades in the iron-copper sulphate reaction.

topics.meaning: A double displacement reaction is one in which two compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In a double displacement reaction two reactant solutions exchange their ions to form two new compounds. When sodium sulphate solution is mixed with barium chloride solution, a white insoluble substance called barium sulphate is formed along with sodium chloride. The insoluble solid that separates out is called a precipitate, and such reactions are also called precipitation reactions. The exchange of ions between the two reactants is the key feature of these reactions. Reactions between acids and bases are also examples of double displacement.
topics.easyExplanation: In a double displacement reaction, two compounds swap their parts to make two new compounds, often forming a solid precipitate.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Mix two solutions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Combine two compounds in solution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Exchange ions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The positive and negative ions swap partners.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form new compounds
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two new compounds are produced.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe precipitate
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: An insoluble solid may form as a precipitate.

examples: Na2SO4 + BaCl2 -> BaSO4 (white precipitate) + 2NaCl.
Pb(NO3)2 + 2KI -> PbI2 (yellow precipitate) + 2KNO3.

importantKeywords.definition: A reaction where two compounds exchange ions.
A reaction that produces an insoluble precipitate.
A substance that does not dissolve in water.

topics.quickSummary: A double displacement reaction involves the exchange of ions between two compounds to form two new products, often including an insoluble precipitate.

topics.meaning: Oxidation is the gain of oxygen or loss of hydrogen, and reduction is the loss of oxygen or gain of hydrogen.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When a substance gains oxygen or loses hydrogen during a reaction it is said to be oxidised, and when it loses oxygen or gains hydrogen it is said to be reduced. When copper is heated in air the surface gets coated with black copper oxide because copper is oxidised; if hydrogen gas is passed over this oxide, the copper is reduced back. Reactions in which one reactant is oxidised while another is reduced at the same time are called oxidation-reduction or redox reactions. The reactivity of substances helps explain these changes. Redox reactions are central to many everyday processes.
topics.easyExplanation: Oxidation means gaining oxygen or losing hydrogen, and reduction means losing oxygen or gaining hydrogen; when both happen together it is a redox reaction.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify oxidation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find the substance that gains oxygen or loses hydrogen.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify reduction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find the substance that loses oxygen or gains hydrogen.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pair the changes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Note that oxidation and reduction occur together.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Name the reaction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If both occur, it is a redox reaction.

examples: 2Cu + O2 -> 2CuO; copper is oxidised to copper oxide.
CuO + H2 -> Cu + H2O; copper oxide is reduced and hydrogen is oxidised.

importantKeywords.definition: Gain of oxygen or loss of hydrogen by a substance.
Loss of oxygen or gain of hydrogen by a substance.
A reaction in which oxidation and reduction occur together.

topics.quickSummary: Oxidation is gain of oxygen or loss of hydrogen and reduction is the opposite; when both occur simultaneously in one reaction it is called a redox reaction.
revisionNotes: Copper heated in air forms black CuO.

topics.topic: Effects of Oxidation: Corrosion and Rancidity
topics.meaning: Corrosion is the slow eating away of metals by air and moisture, and rancidity is the spoiling of oily food by oxidation.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Corrosion is the process in which metals are slowly eaten up by the action of air, moisture, or chemicals on their surface. Iron rusts to form a brown flaky substance, silver develops a black coating, and copper develops a green coat over time. Corrosion damages car bodies, bridges, iron railings, ships, and metal objects, causing great economic loss. Rancidity occurs when fats and oils in food are oxidised, making the food smell and taste bad. Rancidity can be slowed by adding antioxidants, storing food in airtight containers, refrigerating, and flushing packets with nitrogen gas.
topics.easyExplanation: Corrosion is when metals get damaged by air and moisture, like iron rusting, while rancidity is when oily food goes bad because of oxidation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand corrosion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Metals react with air and moisture and get eaten away.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See examples of corrosion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Iron rusts, silver blackens, copper turns green.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand rancidity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Oils and fats in food are oxidised and spoil.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Prevent rancidity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use antioxidants, airtight packing, refrigeration, or nitrogen gas.

examples: Iron objects rust and turn brown when left in humid air.
Chips packets are flushed with nitrogen to prevent rancidity.

importantKeywords.definition: The slow eating away of a metal by air and moisture.
Spoiling of fatty foods by oxidation.
A substance added to food to prevent oxidation.

topics.quickSummary: Corrosion slowly destroys metals through reaction with air and moisture, while rancidity spoils fatty foods by oxidation; both are harmful effects of oxidation that can be controlled.

topics.meaning: Acids are sour-tasting substances that turn blue litmus red, and bases are bitter substances that turn red litmus blue.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Acids and bases can be identified by their effect on indicators. Acids are sour in taste and turn blue litmus red, while bases are bitter, feel soapy, and turn red litmus blue. Common acids include hydrochloric acid in the stomach, citric acid in lemons, and acetic acid in vinegar, while common bases include sodium hydroxide and calcium hydroxide. Acids and bases neutralise each other when mixed. Their chemical behaviour with metals, metal carbonates, and metal hydrogencarbonates helps confirm whether a substance is acidic or basic.
topics.easyExplanation: Acids taste sour and turn blue litmus red, while bases taste bitter, feel soapy, and turn red litmus blue.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test with litmus
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Acids turn blue litmus red; bases turn red litmus blue.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note the taste
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Acids are sour and bases are bitter.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check the feel
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Bases feel soapy to the touch.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe neutralisation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Acids and bases cancel each other when mixed.

examples: Lemon juice contains citric acid and tastes sour.
Soap and baking soda solutions are basic and feel slippery.

importantKeywords.definition: A sour substance that turns blue litmus red.
A bitter substance that turns red litmus blue.
A natural indicator obtained from lichens.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Litmus paper detects acids and bases.

topics.quickSummary: Acids are sour and turn blue litmus red while bases are bitter and turn red litmus blue, and the two neutralise each other when combined.
revisionNotes: Acids: sour, turn blue litmus red.
Bases: bitter, turn red litmus blue.
Acids and bases neutralise each other.

topics.meaning: Indicators are substances that show whether a solution is acidic or basic by a change in colour or smell.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Indicators are substances that tell us whether a substance is acidic or basic by changing colour. Litmus, obtained from lichens, is the most common natural indicator; it is purple in distilled water, turning red in acids and blue in bases. Other natural indicators include red cabbage juice and the coloured petals of flowers like Hydrangea, Petunia, and Geranium. Some substances change their odour in acidic or basic media and are called olfactory indicators, such as onion and vanilla. Synthetic indicators like methyl orange and phenolphthalein are also used to detect acids and bases.
topics.easyExplanation: Indicators are substances that change colour or smell to tell us if something is an acid or a base.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add the indicator
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Put a few drops or a strip into the test solution.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Watch the colour
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Note whether the colour matches acid or base.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compare results
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Litmus, phenolphthalein, and methyl orange give known colours.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use olfactory indicators
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some indicators change smell instead of colour.

examples: Phenolphthalein stays colourless in acid but turns pink in base.
Onion smell vanishes when treated with a base, acting as an olfactory indicator.

importantKeywords.definition: A substance that signals acidic or basic nature by a change.
An indicator whose smell changes in acids or bases.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Vanilla loses its smell in a base.

importantKeywords.definition: A synthetic indicator, colourless in acid and pink in base.

topics.quickSummary: Indicators reveal whether a solution is acidic or basic through changes in colour or odour, with litmus, phenolphthalein, methyl orange, and olfactory indicators being common examples.
revisionNotes: Phenolphthalein: colourless in acid, pink in base.
Methyl orange is a synthetic indicator.
Onion and vanilla are olfactory indicators.

topics.topic: Reactions of Acids and Bases with Metals
topics.meaning: Acids react with metals to release hydrogen gas, and some bases also react with metals to release hydrogen.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When an acid reacts with a metal, hydrogen gas is liberated and a corresponding salt is formed; for example zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid to give zinc sulphate and hydrogen. The hydrogen gas produced burns with a pop sound when a burning candle is brought near it. Bases can also react with certain metals; for instance sodium hydroxide reacts with zinc to form sodium zincate and release hydrogen gas. This shows that not only acids but also some bases can produce hydrogen gas with metals. The general rule for acids is that acid plus metal gives salt plus hydrogen.
topics.easyExplanation: Acids react with metals to give a salt and hydrogen gas, and some bases also release hydrogen when they react with metals.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add acid to metal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pour dilute acid over a reactive metal like zinc.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe bubbles
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Hydrogen gas is released as bubbles.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test the gas
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A burning candle makes a pop sound with hydrogen.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note base reactions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some bases like NaOH also give hydrogen with metals.

examples: Zn + H2SO4 -> ZnSO4 + H2 gas with a pop sound.
2NaOH + Zn -> Na2ZnO2 + H2.

importantKeywords.definition: A compound formed when an acid reacts with a metal or base.
A light gas that burns with a pop sound.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Released when zinc reacts with acid.

importantKeywords.definition: Testing hydrogen gas by a pop sound with a flame.

topics.quickSummary: Acids react with metals to give a salt and hydrogen gas, and some bases such as sodium hydroxide also react with metals to release hydrogen, identified by a pop sound.
revisionNotes: Hydrogen burns with a pop sound.
Zinc with H2SO4 gives zinc sulphate.

topics.topic: Reactions of Acids with Carbonates and Bases
topics.meaning: Acids react with metal carbonates and hydrogencarbonates to give carbon dioxide, and with bases to give salt and water.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Metal carbonates and metal hydrogencarbonates react with acids to give the corresponding salt, water, and carbon dioxide gas. When this carbon dioxide is passed through limewater, the limewater turns milky due to the formation of calcium carbonate, which is the standard test for carbon dioxide. Acids also react with bases in a neutralisation reaction to form salt and water; for example, sodium hydroxide reacts with hydrochloric acid to form sodium chloride and water. Metal oxides are basic in nature and react with acids to form salt and water as well. These reactions show the close relationship between acids and bases.
topics.easyExplanation: Acids react with carbonates to give carbon dioxide gas, and react with bases to form salt and water in neutralisation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: React acid with carbonate
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Acid plus carbonate gives salt, water, and carbon dioxide.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test for CO2
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pass the gas into limewater; it turns milky.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Neutralise with base
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Acid plus base gives salt and water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: React with metal oxide
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Metal oxides are basic and form salt and water with acids.

examples: Na2CO3 + 2HCl -> 2NaCl + H2O + CO2; limewater turns milky.
NaOH + HCl -> NaCl + H2O is neutralisation.

importantKeywords.definition: Reaction of an acid with a base to give salt and water.
Passing CO2 into limewater turns it milky.
A basic oxide that reacts with acids.

topics.quickSummary: Acids react with carbonates and hydrogencarbonates to produce carbon dioxide, and with bases and metal oxides to form salt and water in neutralisation reactions.

topics.topic: Acids and Bases in Water Solutions
topics.meaning: Acids release hydrogen ions and bases release hydroxide ions when dissolved in water.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Acids produce hydrogen ions (H+) in water, and it is these hydrogen ions that are responsible for their acidic behaviour; acids do not show acidic character in the absence of water. The hydrogen ion does not exist alone but combines with water to form a hydronium ion (H3O+). Bases that dissolve in water are called alkalis and produce hydroxide ions (OH-) in solution. The process of dissolving an acid or base in water is highly exothermic, so acid must always be added slowly to water and never water to acid, to avoid the mixture splashing out due to excessive heat. This careful procedure prevents accidents.
topics.easyExplanation: Acids give hydrogen ions and bases give hydroxide ions in water; always add acid to water slowly because a lot of heat is released.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Dissolve in water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add the acid or base to water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form ions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Acids give H+ ions; bases give OH- ions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make hydronium
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: H+ combines with water to form H3O+.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Mix safely
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Always add acid to water slowly to control the heat.

examples: HCl in water gives H3O+ and Cl- ions.
NaOH in water gives Na+ and OH- ions.

importantKeywords.definition: The ion H3O+ formed when H+ combines with water.
A base that is soluble in water.
The ion OH- produced by bases in water.

topics.quickSummary: Acids release hydrogen ions and bases release hydroxide ions in water, and because dissolving them is exothermic, acid must always be added slowly to water.
revisionNotes: Acids give H+ (as H3O+) in water.
Always add acid to water, not water to acid.

topics.meaning: The pH scale measures the strength of acids and bases from 0 to 14 based on hydrogen ion concentration.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The pH scale is used to measure how acidic or basic a solution is, with values ranging from 0 to 14. A pH of exactly 7 is neutral, values less than 7 are acidic, and values greater than 7 are basic. The lower the pH, the more acidic the solution, and the higher the pH, the more basic it is. A universal indicator shows different colours at different pH values and is used to find the approximate pH of a solution. The pH scale is important in everyday life, agriculture, and in our bodies, where the survival of organisms depends on a narrow pH range.
topics.easyExplanation: The pH scale runs from 0 to 14; below 7 is acidic, 7 is neutral, and above 7 is basic.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand the scale
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: pH ranges from 0 to 14.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify neutral
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A pH of 7 is neutral, like pure water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify acidic
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Values below 7 are acidic; lower means stronger acid.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify basic
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Values above 7 are basic; higher means stronger base.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use a universal indicator
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It shows colours for different pH values.

examples: Lemon juice has a pH of about 2, showing it is strongly acidic.
A soap solution has a pH above 7, showing it is basic.

importantKeywords.definition: A number measuring acidity or basicity from 0 to 14.
An indicator showing many colours across the pH range.
A solution that is neither acidic nor basic, with pH 7.

topics.quickSummary: The pH scale from 0 to 14 measures hydrogen ion concentration, where below 7 is acidic, 7 is neutral, and above 7 is basic, often read using a universal indicator.
revisionNotes: Universal indicator shows pH by colour.

topics.topic: Importance of pH in Everyday Life
topics.meaning: pH plays a vital role in digestion, tooth decay, plant growth, and how organisms survive.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The pH of our body and surroundings affects many processes in everyday life. Our stomach produces hydrochloric acid that helps digestion, but excess acid causes indigestion which is relieved by antacids that are mild bases. Tooth decay starts when the pH in the mouth falls below 5.5, because acids produced by bacteria attack the enamel; using basic toothpaste neutralises this acid. Plants and animals are sensitive to pH, so soil pH affects crop growth and acid rain with a pH below 5.6 harms aquatic life. Bee stings inject an acid that causes pain, which can be soothed using a mild base like baking soda.
topics.easyExplanation: pH matters in digestion, tooth decay, plant growth, and survival; antacids and toothpaste use bases to fight acids.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Stomach acidity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: HCl aids digestion; excess causes indigestion.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use antacids
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Mild bases neutralise excess stomach acid.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Protect teeth
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Tooth decay begins below pH 5.5; toothpaste neutralises acid.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Soil and rain
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plants need correct soil pH; acid rain harms life.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Treat stings
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Baking soda neutralises acidic bee stings.

examples: Antacids like milk of magnesia relieve acidity by neutralising stomach acid.
Baking soda applied to a bee sting reduces pain by neutralising the acid.

importantKeywords.definition: A mild base that neutralises excess stomach acid.

topics.meaning: A salt is a compound formed when an acid reacts with a base, and salts can be grouped into families by their ions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Salts are formed in neutralisation reactions between an acid and a base. Salts having the same positive or negative ion belong to the same family; for example, sodium chloride and sodium sulphate belong to the sodium family, while sodium chloride and potassium chloride belong to the chloride family. The pH of a salt solution depends on the strength of the acid and base from which it is formed: a salt of a strong acid and a strong base is neutral, a salt of a strong acid and weak base is acidic, and a salt of a weak acid and strong base is basic. Common salt, or sodium chloride, is an important salt obtained from seawater and mines. It is the raw material for making many useful chemicals.
topics.easyExplanation: A salt is made when an acid and a base react, and salts with the same ions form a family.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form a salt
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: An acid reacts with a base to give a salt and water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Group by ion
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Salts sharing an ion form a family.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the pH
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The pH depends on the parent acid and base strength.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note common salt
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sodium chloride is obtained from seawater and mines.

examples: Sodium chloride and sodium sulphate share the sodium ion, forming a family.
Sodium chloride from seawater is the source of many chemicals.

topics.topic: Important Salts and Water of Crystallisation
topics.meaning: Many useful salts such as washing soda, baking soda, bleaching powder, and plaster of Paris are made from common salt, and some salts contain water of crystallisation.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Common salt is the starting material for many important chemicals. Sodium hydroxide is made by passing electricity through brine in the chlor-alkali process, also giving chlorine and hydrogen. Bleaching powder is produced by the action of chlorine on slaked lime and is used to bleach cloth and disinfect water. Baking soda (sodium hydrogencarbonate) is used in cooking and antacids, while washing soda (sodium carbonate) is used for cleaning and softening hard water. Plaster of Paris is made by heating gypsum and sets into a hard mass when mixed with water, and water of crystallisation is the fixed number of water molecules present in one formula unit of a salt, as in copper sulphate pentahydrate.
topics.easyExplanation: Common salt gives us washing soda, baking soda, bleaching powder, and plaster of Paris, and some salts hold fixed water molecules called water of crystallisation.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Both are sodium salts used in homes.

topics.meaning: Metals are generally hard, lustrous, malleable, ductile, sonorous, and good conductors of heat and electricity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Metals have several characteristic physical properties. They have a shiny surface called metallic lustre, are good conductors of heat and electricity, and are usually solid at room temperature. Metals are malleable, meaning they can be beaten into thin sheets, and ductile, meaning they can be drawn into wires; gold and silver are the most malleable and ductile metals. They are sonorous, producing a ringing sound when struck, and most have high melting points. However, there are exceptions: mercury is a liquid metal, sodium and potassium are soft and can be cut with a knife, and lead and mercury are poor conductors of heat.
topics.easyExplanation: Metals are shiny, can be hammered into sheets and drawn into wires, make a ringing sound, and conduct heat and electricity well.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Notice lustre
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Metals have a shiny metallic surface.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test conductivity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Metals conduct heat and electricity well.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Metals can be beaten into sheets and drawn into wires.

topics.meaning: Non-metals are generally dull, brittle, poor conductors, and exist as solids, liquids, or gases.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Non-metals show properties that are largely opposite to those of metals. They are usually dull rather than lustrous, although iodine and diamond are exceptions that have lustre. Non-metals are poor conductors of heat and electricity, with graphite being an important exception that conducts electricity. They are neither malleable nor ductile but are brittle and break easily when solid. Non-metals exist in all three states: bromine is a liquid, carbon and sulphur are solids, and oxygen and nitrogen are gases. Diamond, a form of carbon, is the hardest natural substance and has a very high melting point, showing again that exceptions exist.
topics.easyExplanation: Non-metals are usually dull, brittle, and poor conductors, and can be solids, liquids, or gases.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe appearance
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Non-metals are usually dull, not shiny.

topics.topic: Chemical Reactions of Metals with Air, Water and Acids
topics.meaning: Metals react with oxygen to form basic oxides, with water to give hydrogen, and with acids to release hydrogen gas.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Metals react with oxygen in air to form metal oxides, which are basic in nature; some, like aluminium oxide and zinc oxide, are amphoteric and react with both acids and bases. Different metals react with water at different rates: sodium and potassium react violently with cold water, magnesium reacts with hot water, and iron reacts only with steam. Metals react with dilute acids to produce a salt and hydrogen gas, with the most reactive metals reacting fastest, while copper and silver do not react with dilute acids. Aqua regia, a mixture of concentrated hydrochloric acid and nitric acid, can dissolve even gold and platinum. These reactions show that metals differ widely in reactivity.
topics.easyExplanation: Metals react with air to form basic oxides, with water to release hydrogen, and with acids to give a salt and hydrogen gas.

stepByStepExplanation.title: React with oxygen
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Metals form metal oxides that are usually basic.

stepByStepExplanation.title: React with water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Reactive metals release hydrogen from water or steam.

stepByStepExplanation.title: React with acids
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Metals give a salt and hydrogen gas with dilute acids.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Copper and silver do not react with dilute acids.

examples: 2Na + 2H2O -> 2NaOH + H2; sodium reacts violently with water.

topics.quickSummary: Metals react with oxygen to form basic or amphoteric oxides, with water at varying rates to give hydrogen, and with dilute acids to produce a salt and hydrogen, reflecting their differing reactivity.

topics.meaning: The reactivity series is a list of metals arranged in order of decreasing chemical reactivity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The reactivity series is an arrangement of metals in the order of their decreasing reactivity. Potassium, sodium, calcium, magnesium, and aluminium are highly reactive and appear at the top, while gold, silver, platinum, and copper are least reactive and appear at the bottom. A more reactive metal can displace a less reactive metal from its salt solution, which is the basis of displacement reactions. The position of a metal in the series determines how it is extracted from its ore and how easily it reacts with oxygen, water, and acids. Hydrogen is included in the series as a reference even though it is not a metal.
topics.easyExplanation: The reactivity series lists metals from most reactive to least reactive, and more reactive metals displace less reactive ones.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Arrange the metals
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: List metals from most to least reactive.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Place reactive metals on top
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Potassium and sodium are most reactive.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Place unreactive metals below
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Gold and silver are least reactive.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A higher metal displaces a lower one from its salt.

Gold and silver are at the bottom and remain unreactive in nature.

topics.meaning: Ionic compounds are formed by the transfer of electrons from metals to non-metals, producing oppositely charged ions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When metals react with non-metals, the metal atoms lose electrons to form positively charged ions called cations, and the non-metal atoms gain those electrons to form negatively charged ions called anions. The strong electrostatic force of attraction between these oppositely charged ions holds them together in an ionic compound, such as sodium chloride. Ionic compounds are usually solids that are hard and brittle, have high melting and boiling points, and dissolve in water but not in petrol or kerosene. They do not conduct electricity in the solid state but conduct in the molten or aqueous state because the ions become free to move. These properties arise directly from their strong ionic bonds.
topics.easyExplanation: Ionic compounds form when a metal gives electrons to a non-metal, creating ions that attract each other strongly.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Transfer electrons
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The metal loses electrons; the non-metal gains them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form ions

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Opposite charges attract and bond strongly.

topics.topic: Occurrence of Metals and Their Ores
topics.meaning: Metals occur in nature as free elements or in combined form as minerals and ores from which they are extracted.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Most metals are found in nature in a combined state as compounds, while a few unreactive metals like gold, silver, and platinum occur in a free or native state. The elements or compounds that occur naturally in the earth's crust are called minerals, and a mineral from which a metal can be profitably extracted is called an ore. Metals are often found as oxides, sulphides, or carbonates. The extraction of a metal from its ore depends on the reactivity of the metal, so highly reactive metals require electrolysis while less reactive ones can be obtained by simpler methods. Before extraction, ores are first concentrated to remove impurities called gangue.
topics.easyExplanation: Metals occur free or combined; a mineral from which metal is profitably extracted is called an ore.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the metal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Reactive metals occur combined; unreactive ones occur free.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify minerals
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Natural compounds of metals are minerals.

topics.quickSummary: Metals occur either free or combined as minerals, and those minerals from which metals can be profitably extracted are called ores, which must be concentrated to remove gangue before extraction.
revisionNotes: Reactive metals occur combined; unreactive free.

topics.meaning: Metals are extracted from their ores by methods that depend on the reactivity of the metal.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The method used to extract a metal depends on its position in the reactivity series. Metals low in the series, like mercury and copper, can be obtained simply by heating their ores. Metals in the middle, like iron, zinc, and lead, are usually extracted by reducing their oxides with carbon. Sulphide ores are first converted to oxides by roasting, and carbonate ores are converted to oxides by calcination, before reduction. Highly reactive metals such as sodium, magnesium, and aluminium are extracted by electrolytic reduction, where electricity is passed through their molten compounds. The thermit reaction, using aluminium to reduce iron oxide, is used to join railway tracks.
topics.easyExplanation: Metals are extracted by methods chosen according to their reactivity; reactive metals need electrolysis while less reactive ones can be reduced by carbon or heat.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Roast or calcine the ore
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Convert sulphides and carbonates into oxides.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Extract low reactivity metals

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reduce medium metals
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use carbon to reduce the metal oxide.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pass electricity through molten compounds.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Refine the metal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Purify the extracted metal, often by electrolysis.

examples: Zinc oxide is reduced by carbon to obtain zinc metal.
Aluminium is extracted from molten alumina by electrolytic reduction.

Heating a carbonate ore in limited air to form an oxide.

topics.meaning: Corrosion is the gradual deterioration of metals by reaction with air and moisture, and it can be prevented by various methods.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Corrosion occurs when metals are attacked by substances around them such as moisture and acids. Iron rusts to form a brown layer in the presence of both air and moisture, silver tarnishes black, and copper develops a green coating. Corrosion weakens structures like bridges, ships, and machinery and causes large economic losses. It can be prevented by painting, oiling, greasing, galvanising, anodising, or making alloys. Galvanisation is the process of coating iron or steel with a layer of zinc to protect it from rusting, and this protection works even if the coating is scratched.
topics.easyExplanation: Corrosion is the slow damage of metals by air and moisture, like rusting, and can be prevented by coating, painting, galvanising, or making alloys.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Metals react with air and moisture and deteriorate.

topics.meaning: An alloy is a homogeneous mixture of two or more metals, or a metal and a non-metal, with improved properties.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An alloy is made by mixing a metal with other metals or non-metals to improve its properties. Alloys are usually stronger, more resistant to corrosion, and may have lower melting points than the pure metals. Iron is mixed with carbon to form steel and with chromium and nickel to make stainless steel, which does not rust. Brass is an alloy of copper and zinc, while bronze is an alloy of copper and tin, and both are harder than pure copper. Solder, an alloy of lead and tin, has a low melting point and is used for joining electrical wires. The properties of the parent metal change when an alloy is formed.
topics.easyExplanation: An alloy is a mixture of a metal with other metals or non-metals that gives better strength and resistance than the pure metal.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Mix the metals
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Combine a metal with other metals or non-metals.

topics.meaning: Carbon forms covalent bonds by sharing electrons rather than gaining or losing them.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Carbon has four electrons in its outermost shell and needs four more to complete its octet. It cannot easily gain or lose four electrons, so instead it shares its electrons with other atoms to form covalent bonds. A covalent bond is formed by the sharing of a pair of electrons between two atoms. Carbon forms strong covalent bonds with other carbon atoms and with elements like hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and chlorine. Because covalent compounds are formed by sharing electrons, they have low melting and boiling points and are generally poor conductors of electricity since they do not contain ions.
topics.easyExplanation: Carbon shares its four outer electrons with other atoms to form covalent bonds instead of giving or taking electrons.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Carbon has four electrons in its outer shell.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Shared pairs hold atoms together as molecules.

In a water molecule, oxygen shares electrons with two hydrogen atoms.

topics.meaning: Catenation is carbon's ability to bond with other carbon atoms, and tetravalency is its capacity to form four bonds.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Carbon shows two special properties that allow it to form a very large number of compounds. The first is catenation, the ability of carbon atoms to link with other carbon atoms to form long chains, branched chains, and rings. The second is tetravalency, meaning carbon has a valency of four and can bond with four other atoms of carbon or some other element. Because carbon is small, the bonds it forms are strong and stable. These two properties together explain why carbon forms millions of compounds, far more than any other element. The strength of carbon-carbon bonds makes these compounds quite stable.
topics.easyExplanation: Carbon can join with many other carbon atoms (catenation) and can form four bonds at once (tetravalency), letting it make millions of compounds.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Carbon atoms link to form chains and rings.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Small carbon atoms form strong, stable bonds.

topics.meaning: Saturated hydrocarbons have only single bonds between carbon atoms, while unsaturated ones have double or triple bonds.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Compounds made of only carbon and hydrogen are called hydrocarbons. Saturated hydrocarbons, called alkanes, contain only single bonds between carbon atoms; methane and ethane are examples. Unsaturated hydrocarbons contain at least one double or triple bond between carbon atoms: those with a double bond are called alkenes and those with a triple bond are called alkynes. Ethene has a carbon-carbon double bond and ethyne has a triple bond. Saturated hydrocarbons generally burn with a clean blue flame, while unsaturated hydrocarbons burn with a yellow sooty flame because of incomplete combustion. This difference in flame helps distinguish the two types.
topics.easyExplanation: Saturated hydrocarbons have only single bonds, while unsaturated ones have double or triple bonds between carbon atoms.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify hydrocarbons
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Compounds of only carbon and hydrogen.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find single bonds
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Saturated hydrocarbons (alkanes) have only single bonds.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find multiple bonds
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Unsaturated ones have double (alkenes) or triple (alkynes) bonds.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Saturated burn blue; unsaturated burn with sooty yellow flame.

examples: Methane and ethane are saturated hydrocarbons with only single bonds.

A hydrocarbon with double or triple bonds.

topics.meaning: Allotropes are different forms of the same element, and carbon exists as diamond, graphite, and fullerenes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Carbon exists in several different physical forms called allotropes, which have the same chemical nature but very different physical properties. In diamond, each carbon atom is bonded to four other carbon atoms forming a rigid three-dimensional structure, which makes diamond the hardest known natural substance. In graphite, each carbon atom is bonded to three others in the same plane, forming layers that can slide over each other, so graphite is soft, slippery, and a good conductor of electricity. Fullerenes form another class of carbon allotropes, with C-60 having atoms arranged like a football. These different arrangements explain why the same element can have such different properties.
topics.easyExplanation: Carbon exists in different forms called allotropes, such as hard diamond, soft graphite, and ball-shaped fullerenes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define allotropes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Different physical forms of the same element.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Describe diamond
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Each carbon bonds to four others in a rigid 3D network.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Describe graphite
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Each carbon bonds to three others in sliding layers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Describe fullerenes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Carbon atoms arranged like a football in C-60.

Graphite conducts electricity and is used in pencils and electrodes.

An allotrope of carbon with atoms in a ball shape.

topics.quickSummary: Carbon exists as several allotropes including hard diamond, soft conducting graphite, and football-shaped fullerenes, whose different atomic arrangements give them very different physical properties.
revisionNotes: Allotropes are forms of the same element.

topics.meaning: A functional group is an atom or group giving specific properties to a compound, and a homologous series is a family of such compounds.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In carbon compounds, certain reactive atoms or groups of atoms called functional groups determine the chemical properties of the compound, regardless of the length of the carbon chain. Examples include the hydroxyl group in alcohols, the carboxylic acid group, the aldehyde group, and the halogen group. A homologous series is a group of carbon compounds having the same functional group and similar chemical properties, in which successive members differ by a CH2 unit. As we move up a homologous series, the molecular mass increases steadily and physical properties such as melting and boiling points change gradually. This makes it easier to study and predict the behaviour of carbon compounds.
topics.easyExplanation: A functional group gives a compound its special properties, and a homologous series is a family of compounds with the same functional group.

topics.meaning: Nomenclature is the systematic naming of carbon compounds based on the carbon chain and functional group.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Carbon compounds are named systematically so that the name reveals the structure of the molecule. The name is based on the number of carbon atoms in the chain, using prefixes such as meth for one carbon, eth for two, prop for three, and but for four. A suffix or prefix indicating the functional group is then added, such as -ol for alcohols, -oic acid for carboxylic acids, and -al for aldehydes. For saturated compounds the ending -ane is used, while -ene and -yne show double and triple bonds. For example, a two-carbon alcohol is called ethanol. This systematic method allows chemists to name any carbon compound unambiguously.
topics.easyExplanation: Naming a carbon compound is based on how many carbon atoms it has and which functional group is present.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Count carbon atoms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use prefixes like meth, eth, prop, but.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Join the prefix and suffix to name the compound.

topics.meaning: Carbon compounds undergo combustion, oxidation, addition, and substitution reactions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Carbon compounds show several characteristic chemical reactions. Most carbon compounds burn in air to release heat and light in a process called combustion, producing carbon dioxide and water; this makes them useful as fuels. Some substances called oxidising agents, such as alkaline potassium permanganate, can add oxygen to alcohols to convert them into carboxylic acids in oxidation reactions. Unsaturated hydrocarbons undergo addition reactions, where hydrogen is added across a double or triple bond in the presence of a catalyst like nickel or palladium; this is used to make vegetable ghee from oils. Saturated hydrocarbons undergo substitution reactions, in which one or more hydrogen atoms are replaced by other atoms such as chlorine in the presence of sunlight.
topics.easyExplanation: Carbon compounds burn (combustion), can be oxidised, undergo addition if unsaturated, and undergo substitution if saturated.

topics.meaning: Ethanol is a common alcohol and ethanoic acid is a carboxylic acid, both important carbon compounds with distinct properties.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Ethanol, commonly called alcohol, is a liquid at room temperature and is used in alcoholic drinks, medicines, and as a fuel. It reacts with sodium to release hydrogen gas, and on heating with concentrated sulphuric acid it gives ethene through dehydration. Ethanoic acid, commonly called acetic acid, is present in vinegar as a 5 to 8 percent solution; its pure form is called glacial acetic acid because it freezes at a temperature near room temperature. Ethanoic acid reacts with bases to form salts and with carbonates to release carbon dioxide. When ethanol reacts with ethanoic acid in the presence of an acid catalyst, it forms a sweet-smelling ester in a reaction called esterification.
topics.easyExplanation: Ethanol is alcohol used in drinks and fuel, and ethanoic acid is the acid in vinegar; together they form sweet-smelling esters.

topics.meaning: Soaps and detergents are cleansing agents that remove dirt and grease by forming structures called micelles.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Soaps are sodium or potassium salts of long-chain carboxylic acids, while detergents are made from a different kind of cleansing molecule. A soap molecule has two ends: one end is hydrophilic and dissolves in water, while the other end is hydrophobic and dissolves in oil and grease. When soap is added to water containing oily dirt, the hydrophobic ends attach to the dirt while the hydrophilic ends point outward, forming clusters called micelles that lift the dirt away into the water. Soaps do not work well in hard water because they form an insoluble scum with the calcium and magnesium salts present. Detergents do not form scum in hard water, so they can be used even where the water is hard.
topics.easyExplanation: Soaps and detergents clean by surrounding dirt and grease in clusters called micelles; detergents also work in hard water.

topics.meaning: Life processes are the basic functions that living organisms perform to maintain and continue life.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The processes that together perform the function of maintenance and keep an organism alive are called life processes. These include nutrition, respiration, transportation, and excretion. Nutrition provides the energy and materials needed by the body, while respiration releases this energy from food. Transportation moves materials within the body, and excretion removes harmful waste products. Even when an organism is not moving visibly, these processes continue to keep it alive, and the maintenance of these processes is necessary to prevent damage and breakdown of the living structure.
topics.easyExplanation: Life processes are the essential activities like nutrition, respiration, transport, and excretion that keep living things alive.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Functions that maintain and continue life.

topics.meaning: Autotrophic nutrition is the process by which organisms make their own food, mainly through photosynthesis.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In autotrophic nutrition, organisms such as green plants make their own food using simple inorganic materials. Photosynthesis is the process by which plants use carbon dioxide and water in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll to produce carbohydrates and oxygen. The energy from sunlight is absorbed by the green pigment chlorophyll present in chloroplasts. The carbohydrate produced is stored as starch, and the oxygen released is given out into the air. The main events of photosynthesis include absorption of light energy, conversion of light energy to chemical energy with splitting of water, and reduction of carbon dioxide to carbohydrates. Stomata on the leaf surface allow the exchange of gases needed for photosynthesis.
topics.easyExplanation: Autotrophs like green plants make their own food by photosynthesis, using sunlight, carbon dioxide, water, and chlorophyll.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Absorb light
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Chlorophyll absorbs energy from sunlight.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Take in raw materials
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plants take carbon dioxide and water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Split water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Light energy splits water and is converted to chemical energy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make carbohydrates

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Oxygen is given out and starch is stored.

topics.meaning: Heterotrophic nutrition is the mode in which organisms obtain food from other organisms because they cannot make their own.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Organisms that cannot synthesise their own food and depend on other organisms for it show heterotrophic nutrition. There are different types: saprotrophic nutrition, in which organisms like fungi feed on dead and decaying matter; parasitic nutrition, in which organisms like Cuscuta and ticks live on or in a host and take its food; and holozoic nutrition, in which organisms like Amoeba and humans take in whole food and digest it inside the body. Amoeba engulfs food using temporary finger-like extensions called pseudopodia and digests it in a food vacuole. The strategy each organism uses depends on the form and availability of its food.
topics.easyExplanation: Heterotrophs cannot make their own food and get it from other organisms by saprotrophic, parasitic, or holozoic nutrition.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define heterotrophs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Organisms that depend on others for food.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Living on a host and taking its food.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Taking in and digesting whole food, as in Amoeba.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Amoeba uses pseudopodia to take in food.

examples: Fungi like bread mould feed saprotrophically on decaying bread.

topics.meaning: Human nutrition involves taking in food and digesting it through the alimentary canal with the help of enzymes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In human beings, food passes through a long tube called the alimentary canal, which begins at the mouth and ends at the anus. Digestion begins in the mouth, where teeth break food and saliva containing the enzyme salivary amylase begins to break down starch. The food then moves through the food pipe to the stomach, where gastric juice containing hydrochloric acid, the enzyme pepsin, and mucus acts on proteins. In the small intestine, bile from the liver and juices from the pancreas and intestinal walls complete the digestion of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. The digested food is absorbed by finger-like projections called villi in the small intestine, and undigested waste is removed through the anus.
topics.easyExplanation: In humans, food is digested as it moves through the alimentary canal, broken down by enzymes and absorbed in the small intestine.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Teeth and salivary amylase start digesting starch.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pass to the stomach

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Bile and pancreatic juice complete digestion.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Villi absorb digested food into the blood.

topics.meaning: Respiration is the process of breaking down food to release energy, and it can be aerobic or anaerobic.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Respiration is the process in which food, mainly glucose, is broken down to release energy for the body. In aerobic respiration, glucose is completely broken down in the presence of oxygen to give carbon dioxide and water, releasing a large amount of energy. In anaerobic respiration, which occurs in the absence of oxygen, glucose is broken down only partially; in yeast it gives ethanol and carbon dioxide, while in our muscles during vigorous exercise it gives lactic acid, which causes cramps. The energy released during respiration is stored in a molecule called ATP, which acts as the energy currency of the cell. In humans, breakdown of glucose occurs first in the cytoplasm and then in the mitochondria.
topics.easyExplanation: Respiration breaks down glucose to release energy, using oxygen in aerobic respiration and producing lactic acid or ethanol in anaerobic respiration.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Glucose is broken down to release energy.

topics.meaning: The human respiratory system takes in oxygen and removes carbon dioxide through the lungs.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In human beings, air is taken in through the nostrils, where it is filtered, warmed, and moistened before passing through the throat into the windpipe or trachea. Rings of cartilage in the trachea keep the air passage open. The trachea divides into two bronchi that enter the lungs and branch into smaller tubes ending in tiny balloon-like structures called alveoli. The alveoli provide a large surface area for the exchange of gases, where oxygen from the air passes into the blood and carbon dioxide passes out. The respiratory pigment haemoglobin in red blood cells carries oxygen to the tissues, since oxygen is poorly soluble in water and must be transported efficiently.
topics.easyExplanation: In humans, air enters through the nostrils, reaches the lungs, and exchanges gases in tiny air sacs called alveoli.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Air enters through the nostrils and is filtered.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Bronchi branch into tiny air sacs called alveoli.

Haemoglobin in red blood cells carries oxygen from the lungs to tissues.

topics.meaning: Transportation in humans is carried out by the circulatory system, which moves blood through the heart and blood vessels.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Blood is the main fluid that transports food, oxygen, carbon dioxide, and wastes in the human body. The heart is a muscular organ with four chambers that pumps blood: the two upper chambers are atria and the two lower chambers are ventricles. Oxygen-rich blood from the lungs enters the left atrium and is pumped by the left ventricle to the body, while oxygen-poor blood from the body enters the right atrium and is pumped to the lungs. This separation of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood ensures an efficient supply of oxygen, and because blood passes through the heart twice in one cycle, it is called double circulation. Arteries carry blood away from the heart, veins carry it back, and valves prevent the backflow of blood.
topics.easyExplanation: In humans, the heart pumps blood through arteries and veins, keeping oxygen-rich and oxygen-poor blood separate in double circulation.

topics.meaning: Plants transport water and minerals through xylem and food through phloem.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Plants have two separate transport systems made of conducting tissues. Xylem transports water and dissolved minerals upward from the roots to the leaves and other parts of the plant. The upward movement of water is aided by transpiration, the loss of water vapour from leaves through stomata, which creates a suction pull. Phloem transports food, mainly sucrose made during photosynthesis, from the leaves to other parts of the plant, including roots, fruits, and growing tissues; this movement of food is called translocation. Unlike water transport, translocation through phloem requires energy. These two systems together meet the transport needs of the plant.
topics.easyExplanation: Plants move water and minerals up through xylem and food through phloem to all parts of the plant.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Roots absorb water and minerals from the soil.

topics.meaning: Excretion is the removal of harmful nitrogenous wastes from the body, carried out mainly by the kidneys.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Excretion is the biological process of removing harmful metabolic wastes, such as nitrogenous compounds like urea, from the body. In human beings the main excretory organs are a pair of kidneys, along with the ureters, the urinary bladder, and the urethra. Each kidney contains many tiny filtering units called nephrons that filter the blood, removing wastes while reabsorbing useful substances like glucose, amino acids, and water. The filtered waste, called urine, passes through the ureters to the bladder, where it is stored until it is released through the urethra. When the kidneys fail, a process called dialysis is used to remove wastes from the blood artificially.
topics.easyExplanation: Excretion removes wastes like urea from the body, mainly through the kidneys, which filter the blood and make urine.

topics.meaning: Control and coordination are the processes by which organisms respond to changes in their environment in a controlled way.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Living organisms respond to changes in their environment, and these responses must be carefully controlled and coordinated. In animals, control and coordination are carried out by the nervous system and the hormonal or endocrine system working together. The nervous system provides rapid responses through electrical impulses, while the endocrine system provides slower, longer-lasting responses through chemicals called hormones. Stimuli are changes in the environment to which organisms react. Movements in response to stimuli, such as a child quickly removing a hand from a hot object, show coordination between different parts of the body. This careful control prevents harm and helps the organism survive.
topics.easyExplanation: Control and coordination let organisms respond to their surroundings, managed by the nervous and hormonal systems in animals.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Organisms sense changes in the environment.

topics.meaning: The nervous system is made of neurons that carry information as electrical impulses throughout the body.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The nervous system is a network of specialised cells called neurons or nerve cells, which are the basic units of the system. A neuron has three main parts: dendrites that receive information, a cell body, and a long axon along which the impulse travels. Information acquired at the end of the dendritic tip sets off a chemical reaction that creates an electrical impulse, which travels along the neuron to its end. At the gap between two neurons, called a synapse, the electrical impulse causes the release of chemicals that pass the signal to the next neuron. This allows information to travel quickly from one part of the body to another and is the basis of nervous coordination.
topics.easyExplanation: The nervous system is made of nerve cells called neurons that carry messages as electrical impulses across the body.

topics.meaning: A reflex action is a quick, automatic response to a stimulus that does not involve conscious thought.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A reflex action is a sudden and automatic response to a stimulus, such as quickly pulling back the hand on touching a hot object. These responses must be made very quickly, so the body uses a shorter pathway called the reflex arc instead of involving the brain in the decision. In a reflex arc, the message travels from the receptor through a sensory neuron to the spinal cord and back through a motor neuron to the muscle, which then responds. This rapid pathway through the spinal cord allows the body to react before the brain has time to process the situation. Reflex actions protect us from harm in dangerous situations.
topics.easyExplanation: A reflex action is a fast, automatic response to a stimulus, like pulling away from a hot object, controlled by the spinal cord.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A receptor senses something like heat.

examples: Pulling the hand back quickly when touching a hot pan is a reflex action.

topics.meaning: The human brain is the main coordinating centre of the body, divided into the forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The brain is the main coordinating centre of the body and is protected inside a bony box called the cranium. It is divided into three major regions: the forebrain, the midbrain, and the hindbrain. The forebrain is the main thinking part and includes regions for hearing, smell, sight, and other sensations, as well as for voluntary actions. The hindbrain includes the cerebellum, which controls posture and balance and the precision of voluntary actions, and the medulla, which controls involuntary actions such as blood pressure, salivation, and vomiting. The brain and spinal cord together form the central nervous system, which receives information and sends instructions to muscles.
topics.easyExplanation: The human brain is the main control centre, made of the forebrain for thinking, and the hindbrain for balance and involuntary actions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Protect the brain
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The brain is protected by the bony cranium.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use the forebrain
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The forebrain handles thinking and senses.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use the cerebellum
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The cerebellum controls balance and precise movement.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use the medulla
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The medulla controls involuntary actions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Coordinate the body
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The brain receives information and directs responses.

examples: The cerebellum helps a person walk in a straight line and maintain balance.
The medulla controls involuntary actions like heartbeat and breathing.

The part of the hindbrain controlling balance.

topics.quickSummary: The human brain, protected by the cranium, is divided into the forebrain for thinking and sensation, and the hindbrain whose cerebellum controls balance and medulla controls involuntary actions.
